summary: The agent spent a very large fraction of tokens re-sending large file contents, tool commands, and long embedded payloads to the LLM across multiple LLM calls. Much of that input appears duplicated across spans (same large prompt blocks and tool payloads), producing heavy avoidable cost with little extra progress per repeat.

redundancy_findings.description: Very large LLM inputs containing nearly-complete file contents / long embedded scripts were re-sent across multiple LLM spans (large repeated prompt payloads and tool command blocks).
redundancy_findings.repeated_target: embedded file contents / long bash commands / scratchpad JSON payloads

redundancy_findings.description: Multiple 'Read' / 'Edit' / 'Write' tool spans returned full file contents which were then embedded into subsequent LLM messages rather than summarized or diffs being used.
redundancy_findings.repeated_target: reads of project files (e.g. scripts/sync_from_logfood.mjs, client pages, server routes)

redundancy_findings.description: Large cache creation tokens (ephemeral cache) were repeatedly created and read across LLM iterations, indicating repeated re-ingestion of large context instead of re-using compact summaries.

failure_modes.title: Re-embedding full file contents instead of diffs or summaries
failure_modes.description: The agent repeatedly asks the LLM to process full file contents (and large bash command blocks) across LLM calls, rather than sending concise diffs or summaries. This produces huge, repeated input tokens that do not proportionally increase progress.

failure_modes.title: Large cache_creation and cache_read churn
failure_modes.description: Spans show very large ephemeral cache_creation_input_tokens and cache_read_input_tokens (e.g. 166,524 ephemeral_1h_input_tokens in some iterations), indicating the system is creating large caches and immediately re-reading them across iterations instead of using compact representations.

failure_modes.title: Repeated tool-use patterns re-run instead of incremental
failure_modes.description: The agent runs many tool Bash/Read/Edit/Write sequences multiple times with the same commands or file paths, which looks like redoing work or re-verifying identical information rather than using previous results.

recommendations: Avoid re-sending full file contents to the model. Instead: (a) send concise summaries, diffs/patches, or only the changed sections; (b) store and reference compact embeddings or concise metadata rather than raw full files.
Use tool responses (Read) as canonical truths and pass only necessary excerpts to LLMs. If LLM needs context from a file, send a short header + exact line ranges rather than the entire file.

Use persistent compact summaries (e.g., 1-2 paragraph compaction) and re-use them across LLM steps rather than repeatedly recreating ephemeral caches. Where large ephemeral caches are created, avoid re-creating them per-iteration.
When orchestrating multi-step tool runs, avoid re-running identical diagnostic commands; cache results of successful tool runs and reference the cached result IDs instead of re-invoking the tool.

Implement token-aware heuristics: stop embedding long tool command blocks and huge scratchpad JSON directly into LLM messages; instead store them in a tool result and only pass a short pointer and the few lines needed.
raw_report:
I inspected the trace using the dataset overview, trace search, and view_spans as required. Below is my audit focused on token efficiency and quality.

Key high-level findings (short):
- The run consumed ~479k input tokens and ~70k output tokens (dataset rollup). Large LLM spans repeatedly re-embedded long code/files and many tool calls, producing heavy input-side token spend that often appears avoidable.
- Most costly spans are LLM spans that re-used large cached contexts and also re-read files via tool Reads; many spans show repeated large identical payloads (same file contents or same batch of tool instructions) across multiple LLM calls.
- No OTel error-status spans, but high redundancy and repeated large cache creations / cache reads indicate token waste and some inefficient plan structure.

Now the required JSON verdict (with evidence and recommendations).
